sentences.text: Right.
Right.
Right.
Seder was our own immediate family, my mother, my sister, my brother, and my father.
The family celebrated-- everybody celebrated the seder more or less in his house, so we didn't have guests for the Passover seder.
But the seder itself was celebrated at length.
The whole Haggadah, the story, was told, with emphasis.
And I-- I was encouraged to add to it.
In other words, the-- the Haggadah encourages everybody to tell more and more about the story of Egypt and the exodus from Egypt.
It says so in the Haggadah.
And as a result, I would prepare a few stories just from my own initiative by reading about it and I would add to the Haggadah.
My brother would too.
That was encouraged.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say the seder lasted probably to midnight, because there was so much to tell and dwell into that and read everything with emphasis and so forth.
Of course, as a child, I was told that Elijah is coming to every house, sipping from the glass of wine.
And I believed it until a certain age.
Well, there were two kinds of matzahs.
There was plain matzah, which is sort of like the Manischewitz matzah here, which was baked in Tarnow.
But there was another matzah, which was called shemurah matzah.
That matzah was-- was hand-baked.
And it was supervised from the moment that the wheat was cut and harvested in the field, to make sure that the grains do not ferment in any way or grow or so forth.
So that we had not much, but one or two matzahs like that, which came directly from the rabbi.
So-- but there were two kinds of matzahs.
In other words, in some households, they would only eat the shemurah matzah, which was not very tasty and not very easily digestible, as a matter of fact.
But we had both kinds, so that just shows that we were already, so to speak-- my father was not that Orthodox to insist that we all eat only the shemurah matzah.
But my uncle, in his household, I know they would only eat shemurah matzah during Passover.
In the house, we spoke Yiddish.
The parents to us spoke Yiddish.
But they spoke to my sister Polish.
And that was a phenomenon which repeated itself again and again.
It was a rather strange phenomenon.
We spoke a lot of Polish, but not in the house.
The parents amongst themselves spoke Yiddish.
They spoke to the children, to the sons, Yiddish.
But for some reason, to my sister they spoke Polish.
I don't know how to explain that, but we were not the only ones.
This was quite common.
Well, Yiddish was-- was also sort of part of the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] part of the indoctrination to the Torah was Yiddish.
Boys would be spoken to Yiddish because they also studied their Torah, part of it.
I studied Hebrew and I studied also Yiddish, you know, because studying involved reading a sentence in Hebrew and translating into Yiddish, or else into Hebrew.
I had both ways.

But girls were excluded from that, excused from that and not included in this sort of-- so-- so that may be why they did not acquire that much Yiddish, and because, you know, the maid would speak to her, because to the maid, they obviously had to speak Polish, because she didn't speak Yiddish.
So you know, that's-- I really don't know how to explain that, so-- but that's how it was.
Well, my father was very active in a Jewish Zionistic organization, which was called Mizrachi, you know.
And he donated a lot of money to it.
But his special preoccupation was with the school.
He practically financed the budget of the school.
Whatever was missing money when it came payday for the teachers, you know, and there would not be enough money to pay their salaries, my father would just supplant whatever was missing.
So this was his major preoccupation.
And he also would collect money from others for that purpose, to the extent that he could.
And administrative aspects of the school-- many times the board meeting, so to speak, of the school would be conducted in our own house, because my father was so busy.
He would come back from work.
So the teachers and the school principal would wait for him and they would settle all the administrative problems which were at hand, right there in our living room.
So that was his major preoccupation.
But he was also active in the-- in another area, which was help for the poor, you know.
So in particular, coal, assignment for coal for winter, which was a major problem, because winter were very severe in Tarnow.
And so my father was active in that.
The synagogue to which my father belonged had a very Zionistic Mizrachi type-- religious, Zionistic-type bent.
In other words, it was also a focal point for those activities.
And my father would participate in all those activities.
I belonged to a-- to an organization which was called Ha-Shomer ha-Dati.
It was a religious Zionist organization.
The accent there was on to some extent sport activities, but also lectures and parades and indoctrination in the Zionist idea.
I played soccer.
I played ping pong a lot.
Soccer quite a bit.
We had a radio in our house.
Unfortunately what I am-- what I would like to comment on that is-- and looking back this sounds so strange.
The Jews were very satisfied with making jokes about Hitler, ignoring the threat that Hitler presented.
The jokes about him multiplied.
You could practically hear continuously a new joke about Hitler.
The jokes revolved around the fact that he was supposedly a painter, which he was not.
But, you know, they would make up a joke, painting a room, and people take a comb and put it right in front and act out, you know.
They were ridiculing him.
They're sort of getting even with him by-- by totally ridiculing and making jokes about him.
And they-- and I remember 'til today him proclaiming [SPEAKING GERMAN], which means, 'I will exterminate the Jewry of Europe.' He said that and I heard it over the radio, must have been '35 or '36.
And you would think that people would sit down and seriously contemplate now what are we going to do about that.
Nowhere.
All they did is just created more jokes about him.
And they took out their satisfaction ridiculing him.
I remember that distinctly.
Sounds strange now, but that's what it was.
Well, more or less the same thing.
You know, at one point, we knew even that we didn't have a future in Poland.
In other words, the children were prepared that as we grow up, we have to go someplace in the world.
We have to leave Poland.
We didn't have the option of going to America, because, you know, America wouldn't let you in at that time.
The option to going to Israel was very questionable, because the British wouldn't let you in.
So as best I could guess, I was being prepared that I'll probably have to go to France maybe.
France had a more liberal policy.
It would let in people to study and things like that.
So that colored my looking to the future as far as I was growing up.
But nowhere was there a serious discussion really, which should have been, in the face of what was coming.
In other words, people refuse to face up to that reality that Hitler was presenting.
They refused.
And that is a story which went on throughout the war that repeats itself, that even later on, days before they were so-called resettled-- Hitler called this resettlement.
They never told them that they are taking them to Auschwitz.
They just told them they are resettled.
And people even days before they were resettled believed that they are really being resettled to a labor camp.
This is a-- this part is very important to convey and to educate people about, that Hitler managed to conceal and hide.
He made a strong effort from day one, you know, to cheat and fool people, prevent them from knowing what's ahead.
And for reasons, you know, which might not be so easy to explain, but I think I could explain, is the Jews were willing to accept that lie.
They were willing to fool themselves.

And strangely, when I was in Hungary years later and I tried to tell people, look, this is what happened in Poland, again they would rather fall for false hopes then face the consequences, which meant, for instance, in this specific case running to Romania with me, the way I did.
But that meant abandoning home, everything, and going and suddenly be-- be a, you know--
So in the face of that terrible reality, they prefer to fool themselves.
That's maybe human nature.
And Hitler took great advantage of that throughout.
And starting from those days that you just asked me in Poland, this was prevalent.
Nobody faced up to that reality.

Nobody wanted to, really, because if they had faced up, maybe they didn't have much option, but they probably would have had some options, or at least some people would have had some options and could have saved themselves.
What-- how early was life affected in Tarnow for the Jews by the rise to power of Hitler?
Directly, was not affected.
But anti-Semitism grew stronger and stronger.
In other words, Hitler served sort of like an example for those who call themselves Nationalists.
You see, Hitler's movement was a nationalistic movement.
He called himself National Socialist.
That was the official name of the Nazi Party.
And he had a lot of followers in Poland who were building organizations on the same theme that he was building, you know, nationalism being a strong force all together, even all the way 'til today.
So in that sense, he had an impact on all of Europe, France and Poland, Czechoslovakia, and so forth, that nationalistic parties were springing up all over Europe.
And nationalistic parties meant anti-Semitic parties, because nationalism stresses one nation and is against any ethnic diversity or any ethnic groups.
All nationalists are against-- even in the United States today, that's the same story.
Well, they were Jews, but of Polish extraction.
In other words, anybody whose parent was born in Poland was suddenly expelled out of Germany.
Hitler issued an edict and took all those people, brought them to the border, and told them, you go.
And the Poles, whether they wanted to accept him or not, had no choice, see?
So many German Jews, young people born in Germany but their parents who had been born in Poland, suddenly had to return to Poland, to a place of origin, so to speak.
And many of them came to Tarnow.
And I came in contact with them.
But even that didn't awaken anybody to that reality, see?
Hitler always had an excuse.
In this case, the excuse was that they were actually Polish nationalists.
They never became German citizens.
In other words, they lived in Germany under a special permission for years.
In Europe, this was quite common.

Unlike in this country that an immigrant gets-- becomes a citizen after five years, in Europe the prevailing pattern was that people would live for 20, 30, 40 years as foreign nationals and keep extending their permit to live there.
This was in France, in Belgium, in-- in Germany very prevalent.
Well, they came into town.
We talked to them.
We faced their tragedy.
They were devastated.

For a young boy, say 16 or 17, who didn't speak Polish, who was very much German and wanted to be very much German, as many of the German Jews-- unlike the Polish Jews who-- who were proud of their Jewish identity, the German Jews were different.
They were not-- they were trying to assimilate very strongly.
Right the next generation, the parents would come from Poland but the next generation really wanted to be German very much so.
And suddenly, their Jewish reality was forced upon them.
And so first, they had to cope with that.
For many of them, it was very difficult to accept.
Second, they found themselves in Poland.
And they kind of looked down on Poland, considered this an inferior country, including the Jews of Poland, who considered them inferior to themselves.
And yet here they were, dependent on those people whom they considered inferior to help them.
So it was a-- quite a tragedy, considering the individual German Jew who found himself in that situation.
Plus they were deprived of all their possessions.
Hitler didn't let them take anything with them, barely clothes, you know.
So they had to face great economic difficulties.
So it was a great tragedy for them.
And to some extent [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there is such an expression in Yiddish called [YIDDISH].
That expression implies a person who lacks practicality, who is not adaptable, who is sort of inflexible.
So that-- the Polish Jews, let's put it this way, were different.
They were much more adaptable.
They would get along.
And in the face of great difficulties, they would make up for the difficulties with jokes.
The German Jews-- I'm generalizing, of course-- were more serious.
They took their faith, so, you know, they were heartbroken.
And they find it very difficult to recover from the terrible blow, because it was a great blow for them.
I remember two boys, which-- whom I befriended.
And they were sort of not very open, not very easy to-- to associate with.
I think one-- one's name was Rudolph.
That's what I-- and I don't remember the other's name.
Blond hair, this type of boys.
Well, they didn't go to school.
I don't really know why.
The Polish schools, they couldn't go because they didn't speak Polish.

Maybe they didn't go to the Hebrew schools because they-- you know, the Hebrew schools were parochial schools and tuition was very high, so I-- maybe they didn't want to go to school, because they felt that, you know, [INAUDIBLE] not cope with it and so forth.
And this is '38.
And maybe they hoped in a year or two they'll go to school.
But then war broke out, you know, so--
Well, they organized committees.
And the first effort was primarily to find housing for them.
And they did find housing, which wasn't easy, but they did.
Practically everybody someplace had a room, a roof over his head to sleep.
And with food was easier.
They helped them with food and so forth.
Well, that was more difficult.
That was more difficult.
I really don't know of anything in that area very much.
Some probably found jobs and so forth.
Well, 1939, when war broke out, my father bought a bus and took some of his friends and left with us towards Romania, with the hope of getting to Israel.
He left my mother and my sister home, because the fear was that boys and men will be recruited to the army, you know.
But girls were free of that fear.
So whatever his decision was and why-- maybe the bus was so expensive to buy that bus that he needed enough people to finance it.
Maybe that was the reason.
But anyhow, I find myself in 1939 running towards Romania.
And we almost made it.
We were right there on the Romanian border when the Russians arrived, because Russia and Germany reached an agreement and split Poland into two parts.
See Hitler, shook hands with Stalin so to speak.
Not-- they were not meeting personally, but through their foreign ministers.
And they split into two parts.
And suddenly, we found ourself in Russian hands.
And they went to confiscate the bus.
And my father was claiming, but that's my property.
How can you do that?
Blah, blah, blah.
And they put them against the wall.
They were going to shoot to him.
And only really because we begged that soldier, tried to explain that there is a misunderstanding, he somehow let him go.
But he confiscated the bus, of course.
And at that point, we became refugees in a little town called Kosovo.
And for the next two years, I spent in Russia.
And then I came back to Tarnow in 1941, after the Germans invaded this part of Poland and chased the-- pushed the Russian army back.
I found myself under Hitler.
And this is June 1941, when the German invasion came to this part.
Right.
Well, when we went on the bus, about a month later a man by the name of Edwin Hauser from Vienna appeared one day in my father-- in my mother's house, gave her $1, and gave her a paper to sign.
And with that dollar-- dollar, zloty.
With that zloty, he became the owner of my father's factory.
And at that point, all my mother could do is ask him if he would let her work in that factory, because the Jews had to have a job.
Originally, any job would do.
But subsequently, this factory worked for the army.
And it proved sort of an important place to have a job.
So you could last longer and longer when you maintained a job.

So my father-- my mother worked in that factory as a cook, because the factory also provided meals for its-- the workers were essentially like slave laborers, you know, no rights, no-- the salary was practically none.
But food he had to give people to eat, because otherwise they couldn't work.
So my father-- my mother prepared those meals.
Now, my-- we, me and my brother, came back to Tarnow and also worked in that factory.
My mother asked him if-- to give-- give-- and he agreed to give us jobs too.
We both worked in that factory.
We went back in 1941.
Right.
Right.
Right.
Well--
We left--
We left.
And we were convinced that somehow Poland will withstand the onslaught and Britain and France will not let Hitler invade Poland.
Britain loomed as a very major country.
Britain ruled the world, so to speak.
Britannia ruled the world, had all these colonies, had this greatest navy in the world.
France, you know, was a major power, a major colonial power.
So people imagined that now that finally England and France went to war with Hitler, which is 1939, only after he invaded Poland.
Prior to that, they were tolerating him and appeasing him.
But finally, once they went to war, everybody-- I did-- practically everybody was convinced that Hitler would be defeated in a short period of time, a month, two or three.
And we ran away just to avoid the oncoming onslaught of the Germans, and believing that the Poles sooner or later will manage to group their forces and resist him.
And with the help from the Allies, France and Germany, Hitler will be defeated.
We'll be back home in two months.

Instead came the big surprise that Stalin-- which nobody knew about, it was a shock to everybody, including to the west-- that Stalin behind the scenes reached an agreement with Hitler and they divided Poland.
And that finished that dream up.
My father and my brother.
Yes, we corresponded.
We corresponded.
My father opened up another factory on a very small scale, again making tar-paper.
And under the Russians, there was a system where small factories could exist as cooperatives.
So he organized sort of like a co-operative like that.
And it was semi-nationalized, you know.
There was a-- an office which managed all these little cooperatives, because private enterprise as such was not allowed, so it was basically cooperatives, you know, nationalized.
And we worked in that factory.
Me and my brother worked in the factory.
My father was sort of the technical manager of that factory.

And at one point, the Russians announced that anybody who wants to go back home, meaning for refugees like ourselves who want to go back to their original residence, will be allowed to do though-- to do so.
He has to register.
Approximately nearly close to a million refugees who ran away from Poland like ourselves, mostly Jews, were in that same situation.
Out of those, probably majority, the vast majority registered to go back to Hitler.
Now, that sounds very strange, but there is two explanations for that.
One, as I said before, nobody ever managed to leave Russia, so you had a choice to accept the fact that you'll forever live in Russia or else go back to Germany.
And interestingly enough, again nobody in his faintest imagination imagined that there will be ever a Holocaust like happened later in Germany.
Going back to Germany meant to suffer for two years as a slave laborer and the war will finish sooner or later.
So we together with nearly a million people choose that option and register to go back to Germany.
It was all a big lie.
The Russians never intended to let us back in Germany.
But next-- in two weeks, they went with those lists to all those people who registered at night, grabbed them, and sent them to Siberia.
And this is how 250,000 Jews from Poland ended up in Russia and saved themselves, because they were shipped away to Siberia.
And they spent the rest of the war safely there in Siberia.
We happened to be at that time in a place called Kalusz, which is a little town.
And outside the house where we lived, there was a big haystack.
And when we heard that the soldiers-- we almost saw them-- are rounding up a house down the-- down the road, we all ran into that haystack and hid there.
And we could see the Russian soldiers going through the house with the list, looking for us.
But they were very lazy.
They-- they weren't really very intent on whatever they were doing.
They were just doing it because they were told to do.
Most of the life in Russia was that way.
So anyhow, they looked around, moved around the house, and left.
They didn't bother to climb up on the haystack and look around for more.
This is 1940.
It's about July, August.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After that, there was one boy in our group whose parents were taken away and sent to Russia.
He found himself alone.
So he later went to the [INAUDIBLE], tell them, look, I managed to hide out.
Here I am.
I'm surrender.
They wouldn't send him any more to Russia.
They didn't have transports anymore.
This-- this was the type of Russian management that existed there, everything sort of loose and lax and unexplainable and-- Like in this case, on one hand you were a criminal.
You were being sent to Russia because we were considered traitors.
And on the other hand, you come, you surrender.
You say, the police was looking for me, here I am.
They wouldn't bother him.
They wouldn't send--
So anyhow, subsequently we were all given passports.
In Russia, everybody has to have a passport, which is an identity card.
We were all given passports.
And the passport was stamped with Paragraph 11, which means this person is sort of not to be trusted.
He's an enemy of the state.
And as a result, we were not allowed to live in any large town, only small towns.
And I was not allowed to go to high school, which otherwise I would have been entitled to a free high school education.
Prior to that really, I did go for a short period of time to a Jewish high school.
In Russia.
The language of instruction was Jewish.
In other words, the Poles would go to a school where the language of instruction would be Polish.
Ukrainians would go to a school that was Ukrainian.
And Jews would go to school language of instruction was Yiddish.
And the teacher was from Kiev.
And I remember the way he taught Yiddish, which I thought was funny in a way.
That school I was in Lvov, you see.
But I had to leave Lvov.
And I'm trying to remember why I had to leave Lvov.
So later, I lived in Kalusz.
And that round-up happened in Kalusz.
And subsequently from Kalusz-- we had to leave Kalusz, because Kalusz was considered too big a town for us to live and so forth.
OK.
We'll stop now and continue in a moment.
Good.
